author: @selfcorrected-Bobby
text: mushroom polysccharides are called biopolymers. we have biopolymers like isomaltooliosaccharide (great for liver cancer)... hyaluronic acid of the human body i also a disacharide, the gelatinous cells that helps our bodies retain water, and protect cells in the extracellular matrix..! Mushrooms are great for anticancer (the right ones...!) it's all about the fuel you put in your engine, quite literally, xylitol is best for the pancreas... along with the polysaccharide from the cocos poira mushroom.

author: @selfcorrected-Bobby
text: w0000h00 I love mycelium the psilocybe genera contain psilocybin, which go to the HT2a receptor, the seratonin receptor. They are  seratonin analog and causes an increase in neuronal signalling, which is why it is called the "genius mushroom" or teonanacatl, the flesh of the gods...! It also helps with establishing new neuronal connections and thinking beyond the DMN default mode network...! :) Isn't this an old video?

author: @DiyEcoProjects
text:
HI there, also, you’re video led me on a “Mushroom Trip” this evening lol. 🍄🍄🍄
I was thinking… mushrooms and sawdust block effused with graphene. Copper plate underneath, zinc on top and using a vinegar based electrolyte weak enough not to kill off the mycelium. But then, this is possibly just going to be a galvanic reaction and the plates will eat away over time, no not a perminent thing. Then I saw write ups about MFC’s (mushroom) battery cells. Fascinating!!! Check out MFC's

I love mushrooms. Magic ones have definately helped with depression. Kind of fixed my brain, i can feel it. I get depression cycles for about a week long, but nothing like used to experience. Self development and these darlings im sure helped. Once a year on a winter solstice in a sacred way, tuning in with the land and seeing the dawn, is enough.

Thank you for being a constant inspiration. I hope things are going well for you and that luke is doing ok too. 

All the best, Kieron

author: @DiyEcoProjects
text:
30:10 hi Rob, heres some helpful notes. Ive made Drum skins in the past. 
1.	Once the skin if first taken, put it into a bucket. 
2.	Make a mixture of water + ash + pee (yep) and mix that up and fill the bucket with it.
3.	This mix makes the hair drop out after about a week, need to stur maybe 3 times during that time, every other day.
4.	Put on a flesh beam and scrape the hair off first.
5.	Next, iccy bit is the flat and oil. So what I do is scrape most of the flat off, but then put the skin back into a bucket of SOAPY water to strip off the oils quick. ### I mention this to make the iccy process part that much quicker
6.     Put it back into a bucket with ash and let side for another day. Then rinse with fresh water.
6.	You now have a lovely bit of Rawhide skin (thank you goat, honour to you) to use in making a drum skin. Not a leather process.

Hope that helps
